phrases: Dress modestly when visiting churches and religious sites - cover shoulders and knees
Greet shopkeepers and restaurant staff when entering and leaving establishments
Avoid eating or drinking near fountains and monuments - it's prohibited and can result in fines
safety_tips: Indian Embassy in Rome: Address - Via XX Settembre 5, 00187 Rome | Phone: +39-06-4884642/4884643 | Emergency: +39-348-0101777 | Email: cons.rome@mea.gov.in | Working Hours: Monday-Friday 9:00-17:30
Emergency Numbers: Police (Carabinieri) - 112 | Ambulance/Medical Emergency - 118 | Fire Department - 115 | Tourist Police - 06-46861 | European Emergency Number - 112

Common Scams to Avoid: Fake petition signers near tourist sites who pickpocket while you're distracted | 'Gold ring' scam where someone claims you dropped expensive jewelry | Overcharging at restaurants near tourist areas - always check menu prices | Fake police asking to check your wallet for counterfeit money | Street vendors selling 'designer' goods at inflated prices

Transportation Safety: Use official white taxis with taxi signs and meters | Validate public transport tickets to avoid fines | Be cautious of pickpockets on crowded buses and metro, especially lines A and B | Keep bags in front of you in crowded areas | Avoid unlicensed tour guides and transportation

Money and Document Safety: Keep passport copies separate from originals | Use hotel safes for valuables | Carry minimal cash and use ATMs inside banks when possible | Be aware that some establishments may not accept cards - carry some euros | Report lost/stolen documents to police and embassy immediately

Tourist Area Precautions: Be extra vigilant around Colosseum, Trevi Fountain, Spanish Steps, and Vatican - high pickpocket areas | Avoid street games like 'shell game' or card tricks | Don't accept 'free' bracelets or flowers from street vendors | Be cautious of people dressed as gladiators or centurions demanding payment for photos

Health and Medical: European Health Insurance Card (EHIC) may not cover Indian citizens - ensure travel insurance | Pharmacies (Farmacia) are marked with green cross signs | Tap water is safe to drink | Emergency rooms: Policlinico Umberto I (+39-06-49971) | Bambino Gesù Hospital (+39-06-68591)

General Safety Tips: Stay in well-lit areas at night, especially around Termini Station | Learn basic Italian phrases for emergencies | Keep emergency contacts saved in Italian | Dress modestly when visiting religious sites | Be aware that many shops close during afternoon hours (riposo) | Trust your instincts and avoid situations that feel unsafe

connectivity_tips: Buy a local Italian SIM card from TIM, Vodafone, or WindTre at Rome Fiumicino or Ciampino airports immediately upon arrival - costs around €15-25 for tourist plans with 20-50GB data
Purchase SIM cards from authorized stores like TIM stores, Vodafone shops, or electronics retailers like MediaWorld and Unieuro throughout Rome city center
Consider eSIM options like Airalo, Holafly, or Nomad which offer Italy/Europe plans starting from €4-8 for 1-7 days with 1-10GB data - activate before departure from India
TIM offers the best coverage in Rome and rural areas, followed by Vodafone and WindTre - all three provide excellent 4G/5G coverage in the city
Bring your passport/ID proof when buying physical SIM cards as it's mandatory for registration in Italy
Tourist SIM plans typically include unlimited calls within Italy and to major countries including India, plus generous data allowances
Free WiFi is available at major tourist spots, cafes, and hotels, but having mobile data ensures connectivity while navigating Rome's streets
Download offline maps on Google Maps or Maps.me before traveling to reduce data usage while exploring Rome's attractions
EU roaming regulations don't apply to Indian SIM cards, so avoid using your Indian SIM to prevent bill shock - charges can be ₹500-1000 per day
Tabacchi shops (tobacco stores with 'T' signs) throughout Rome also sell SIM cards and top-up vouchers at competitive prices
For stays longer than 15 days, consider monthly plans from Italian operators which offer better value than tourist-specific packages
Test your SIM card connectivity at the purchase location before leaving to ensure proper activation and network registration

transport_options.description: Rome metro system connects major areas of the city
High-speed trains connect Rome to other Italian cities like Florence
Unlimited access to metro, bus and train travel within Rome
Unlimited access to metro, bus and train travel within Rome

health_tips: No vaccines are required for entry to Italy from India unless you're arriving from a yellow fever endemic area (which India is not).
Recommended routine vaccines: Ensure you're up-to-date with MMR (measles-mumps-rubella), DPT (diphtheria-pertussis-tetanus), chickenpox, polio, and yearly flu shot.
Consider Hepatitis A vaccine if you haven't been vaccinated - recommended for all travelers to Southern Europe, especially if you plan to eat at local establishments.
Hepatitis B vaccine is recommended if you might have intimate contact with locals or need medical procedures.
Typhoid vaccine may be considered if you plan to eat at smaller local establishments or street food, though risk is low in Rome.
Rome's tap water is safe to drink - the city has excellent water quality and numerous public fountains with potable water.
Food safety is generally high in Rome. Stick to busy restaurants and avoid food that's been sitting out for long periods.
Be cautious with gelato and dairy products from smaller vendors during hot summer months - choose busy places with high turnover.
Carry hand sanitizer and use it before eating, especially when trying street food or after using public transport.
Summer heat can be intense (June-August) - stay hydrated, wear sunscreen, and take breaks in shade during midday hours.
Pharmacies (farmacia) are widely available for basic medications. Bring prescription medications in original packaging with a doctor's letter if needed.
European Health Insurance Card (EHIC) won't apply to Indian citizens - ensure you have comprehensive travel insurance covering medical emergencies.
Rome has excellent healthcare facilities. Emergency number is 112. Major hospitals include Policlinico Umberto I and Ospedale San Camillo.
Mosquito-borne diseases are not a significant concern in Rome, but use repellent during summer evenings near the Tiber River if sensitive to bites.
Walking on cobblestones and uneven ancient surfaces is common - bring comfortable, supportive shoes to prevent injuries.
